sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa della escalation delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente. Il VIX è sopra 27, il DXY è stabile intorno a 100.29 e il prezzo del petrolio è sceso del 3.43% a $99.60. La Fed e altre banche centrali stanno monitorando la situazione.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. VIX is above 27, DXY is stable around 100.29, and oil prices have dropped 3.43% to $99.60. The Fed and other central banks are monitoring the situation.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato azionario è in calo: S&P 500 -0.61%, STOXX 600 -0.55%, NIKKEI -0.21%. Il mercato obbligazionario è in tensione: TLT -0.49%, US 10Y Yield +0.28%. Il prezzo dell'oro è in calo del 0.53% a $5025.90. Il mercato valutario è stabile: EUR/USD 1.14.
market_impact_summary.en: Equity markets are down: S&P 500 -0.61%, STOXX 600 -0.55%, NIKKEI -0.21%. Bond market is under pressure: TLT -0.49%, US 10Y Yield +0.28%. Gold price is down 0.53% to $5025.90. FX market is stable: EUR/USD 1.14.

audio_script_it:
Benvenuti al consueto briefing mattutino dei mercati. 

Oggi, 16 marzo 2026, il sentiment di mercato è decisamente risk-off, con un indice Vix che sale sopra 27, riflettendo l'aumento dell'avversione al rischio tra gli investitori. Questo cambio di sentiment è principalmente dovuto all'escalation delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente, dove la situazione sembra peggiorare di giorno in giorno. 

Tra i dati chiave di questa mattina, segnaliamo che il prezzo del petrolio è sceso del 3.43% a $99.60, mentre l'indice del dollaro (DXY) si stabilizza intorno a 100.29. Lo Standard and Poor's 500 ha ceduto lo 0.61%, portandosi a quota 6632 punti. 

Passando agli asset, analizziamo i movimenti più significativi. Il cambio euro dollaro (EUR/USD) è sceso dello 0.73% a 1.14. L'indice del dollaro (DXY) ha perso solo lo 0.07%, mantenendosi stabile. 

Lo Standard and Poor's 500 ha registrato una perdita dello 0.61%, concludendo la sessione a 6632 punti. L'indice Vix, che misura la volatilità del mercato, è salito a 27.19, riflettendo l'aumento dell'incertezza. 

Gli ETF obbligazionari, come il TLT, che traccia i titoli del Tesoro USA a 20 anni, hanno perso lo 0.49%, attestandosi a 86.54. Il rendimento del decennale USA è salito dello 0.28% al 4.28%. 

Sul fronte delle materie prime, l'oro ha perso lo 0.53%, quotando $5025.90 l'oncia. Il prezzo del petrolio Brent è sceso del 3.43% a $99.60 al barile. 

Il Bitcoin, invece, ha guadagnato il 3.53%, attestandosi intorno ai settantamila dollari. L'indice Stoxx seicento ha perso lo 0.55%, concludendo a 59.13. 

L'indice Nikkei ha registrato una perdita modesta dello 0.21%, fermandosi a 53,709. Lo Shanghai Composite ha ceduto lo 0.54%, concludendo la sessione a 4073.32. 

Sul fronte macroeconomico, la BCE e la Federal Reserve saranno protagoniste questa settimana con le loro decisioni sui tassi di interesse. Gli investitori sono particolarmente attenti a qualsiasi segnale che possa indicare come queste banche centrali intendano gestire l'attuale scenario di tensioni geopolitiche e il loro impatto sull'inflazione. 

La BCE, in particolare, dovrà considerare se l'escalation delle tensioni in Medio Oriente possa portare a un aumento dell'inflazione nella zona euro, potenzialmente influenzando la sua politica monetaria. 

La Federal Reserve, dal canto suo, ha già segnalato la sua attenzione verso la situazione, monitorando gli sviluppi per capire se e come intervenire. 

Tra le notizie del giorno, segnaliamo che l'ex presidente degli Stati Uniti, Donald Trump, ha lanciato un avvertimento agli alleati europei in merito alla loro partecipazione alle spese per la difesa della NATO, dichiarando che se non aumenteranno i loro contributi, la NATO potrebbe avere 'un futuro molto brutto'. 

Inoltre, la crisi in Medio Oriente ha riportato al centro dell'attenzione il tema dell'inflazione globale, con molti economisti che temono come l'aumento dei prezzi del petrolio possa avere un impatto significativo sui prezzi al consumo. 

La Cina, che la scorsa settimana ha pubblicato dati economici migliori del previsto, sembra aver ripreso la sua crescita, ma anche Pechino è preoccupata per le tensioni geopolitiche e il loro impatto potenziale sulla sua economia. 

Questa settimana sarà cruciale per capire come le principali banche centrali intendano affrontare la situazione. La Federal Reserve, la BCE, la Banca d'Inghilterra e altre istituzioni finanziarie saranno sotto la lente degli investitori per capire se e come reagiranno ai crescenti timori di inflazione e alle tensioni geopolitiche. 

Per quanto riguarda i dati macroeconomici, gli Stati Uniti attendono con ansia la pubblicazione del PCE Core YoY, prevista per il 28 marzo, e del GDP QoQ, atteso per il 29 aprile. 

In sintesi, il clima di mercato è dominato dalle preoccupazioni geopolitiche e dall'incertezza sul futuro della politica monetaria. Gli investitori sono in attesa delle decisioni delle banche centrali e di ulteriori dati economici per delineare meglio lo scenario futuro. 

Per oggi, merita attenzione il dato sulla Global M2 Liquidity, che ha raggiunto i 22.4 trilioni di dollari, in espansione, con un aumento del 4.3% su base annua. 

Infine, per quanto riguarda i dati specifici da monitorare, segnaliamo che la prossima uscita del PCE Core YoY è prevista per il 28 marzo e il GDP QoQ per il 29 aprile. 

Rimanete sintonizzati per ulteriori aggiornamenti e analisi dettagliate nel corso della giornata.
audio_script_en:
Benvenuti al consueto briefing mattutino dei mercati. 
    Today is March 16, 2026. The market sentiment is risk-off with a score of 7 out of 10 due to escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. This has led to a notable increase in the VIX, which is currently above 27, indicating heightened volatility. The Dollar Index, or DXY, remains stable around 100.29. Meanwhile, oil prices have dropped by 3.43% to $99.60 per barrel.

    Let's take a closer look at the market data. The EUR/USD exchange rate has decreased by 0.73% to 1.14. The S&P 500 has ceded 0.61%, currently standing at 6632.19 points. The VIX, as mentioned, is at 27.19, a slight decrease of 0.37%. The TLT Bond USA 20Y has decreased by 0.49% to 86.54. The US 10-year yield has increased by 0.28% to 4.28%. Gold is trading at $5025.90 per ounce, a decrease of 0.53%. Bitcoin, on the other hand, has seen a significant increase of 3.53%, currently at around $73,730. The Brent oil price has dropped to $99.60 per barrel, a 3.43% decrease. The STOXX 600 has decreased by 0.55% to 59.13, while the NIKKEI has dropped by 0.21% to 53,709. The Shanghai index has also decreased by 0.54% to 4073.32.

    In terms of geopolitics, tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate, directly impacting oil prices and overall market sentiment. The situation is being closely monitored by central banks, including the Federal Reserve. 

    Moving on to macroeconomic data, the last available CPI YoY in the USA was 2.7%, released on February 1, 2026, which is about 43 days ago. The Core CPI YoY was also 2.7%, slightly down from 2.9%. The Non-Farm Payrolls showed a decrease of 92K, and the Unemployment Rate USA increased to 4.4%. The Fed Funds Rate remains at 3.64%.

    Looking ahead, market participants are eagerly awaiting the PCE Core YoY, scheduled for release on March 28, 2026, and the GDP QoQ USA, expected on April 29, 2026. These data points will provide crucial insights into the health of the US economy and potential future actions by the Federal Reserve.

    On the news front, there are significant developments. Former US President Trump has warned European allies about NATO, emphasizing the need for support in the face of the Iran crisis. This has reawakened global inflation fears as the Iran war escalates. Meanwhile, China's economy has shown signs of rebounding, even as the global community grapples with the implications of the Middle East conflict.

    Central banks are set to make crucial rate decisions in the coming days, which will be closely watched for guidance on monetary policy. The situation in the Middle East remains volatile, with significant implications for global markets.

    The UK's Labour Party leader, Starmer, is set to unveil a £50 million energy support package aimed at mitigating the fallout from the Iran war. This move highlights the global effort to address the economic impacts of geopolitical tensions.

    As we move forward, it's essential to monitor several key data releases and events. The market is currently pricing in a risk-off scenario, with safe-haven assets seeing increased demand. The situation in the Middle East will continue to be a focal point, alongside macroeconomic data releases.

    Specifically, investors will be looking at the upcoming PCE Core YoY on March 28 and the GDP QoQ USA on April 29. These releases will provide valuable insights into the trajectory of the US economy and the potential for future interest rate adjustments.

    In conclusion, as we navigate through these uncertain times, staying informed about geopolitical developments and macroeconomic data will be crucial for making informed investment decisions. The risk-off sentiment is likely to persist in the near term, but markets will continue to seek cues from central banks and economic indicators.

    That's all for now. Keep an eye on the developments in the Middle East and the macroeconomic calendar for the next few weeks.

articles.snippet: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Gabriella Gricius, Senior Fellow at the Arctic Institute, to discuss Arctic security, Donald Trump’s ambitions to acquire Greenland, Russia’s and China’s interests in the High North, and how NATO and European capitals are responding.

articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran is entering its third week, with no end yet in sight. Crisis Group experts offer a 360-degree view of its effects on peace, security and economic stability around the world.

He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.title: Middle East Crisis Could Triple Pakistan’s Oil Import Bill
articles.snippet: Previously, we reported that Pakistan could find itself sucked into the Middle East war thanks to the Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement (SMDA) it signed with Saudi Arabia in September 2025. The core of the agreement states that any aggression against one country is considered an aggression against both, potentially requiring Pakistan to intervene against Iran’s missile strikes on Saudi Arabia. However, military confrontation with its southerly neighbor might turn out to be a less existential threat for Pakistan. Surging oil prices are already…

articles.title: How the Iran War Could Reshape Global Energy Policy
articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East have caused the biggest oil disruption in history. The closure of a major transit route between Asia and Europe, as well as restrictions on fossil fuel production in the region, have driven oil and gas prices up, a trend that is set to continue. So, as governments are facing rising consumer energy bills once again, will it be enough to encourage a Covid-era pivot to renewable energy? The U.S. war against Iran has caused the largest oil disruption in history, according to…

articles.title: Jet Fuel Prices Soar as War in Iran Ripples Through Global Aviation
articles.snippet: As the war in Iran spills over into other parts of the Middle East, energy experts expect the price of several oil and gas products to soar over the coming months, driven by shortages. This will likely affect flight prices, with several airlines warning of anticipated price hikes. It could lead to a travel slump, as consumers wait for prices to fall again. Australia’s Qantas Airways, Scandinavia’s SAS, and Air New Zealand are three of the airlines to have already announced airfare hikes in response to the ongoing conflict in the Middle…

articles.title: Oil Prices Remain Volatile as Hormuz Tensions Rise
articles.snippet: Oil markets remained volatile in early Asian trade on Monday as Trump attempts to form a broader coalition of countries to protect shipping through the Strait of Hormuz amid fresh attacks on energy infrastructure across the Middle East. At the time of writing, West Texas Intermediate had dipped 0.53% to $98.19 after an initial surge, while Brent was continuing its climb, rising 0.41% to $103.60 per barrel. The volatility follows a weekend of escalation in the Middle East conflict, beginning on Friday evening when the U.S. bombed military assets…

articles.title: Asia’s LNG Lifeline Takes a Hit
articles.snippet: Asia is the biggest market for liquefied natural gas. Asia is also the destination of up to 90% of Qatari and Emirati LNG—or was, until this month. With the shutdown of Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG complex and the Strait of Hormuz traffic disruption, Asia is facing a lot of energy supply pain. QatarEnergy announced a complete halt to LNG production after Iranian drone strikes hit facilities at Ras Laffan Industrial City and Mesaieed Industrial City on March 2. A force majeure declaration followed on QatarEnergy exports. The move started a…
